title: Two Europes in Five Minutes
content:
Within a few minutes of each other yesterday in Strasbourg, Members of the European Parliament voted through two motions relevant to how we regulate knowledge creation, sharing and application to support research and innovation. Neither has any immediate legal impact, but still set a direction that may well shape future, legislative, measures, for better or for worse.

And that direction is… confused.

The first to be voted on was a report on Generative AI and Copyright, prepared on the initiative of the Parliament’s Legal Affairs Committee, and led by Axel Voss MEP. The second was a
creator: Stephen Wyber

title: The UK’s Copyright Laws Will Hobble Its AI Ambitions
content:
Guest blog post by Benjamin White

Originally published on 11 December 2025 on the Times Higher Education

Artificial intelligence played a crucial role in enabling the safe and rapid development and roll-out of the Covid vaccine. Using scientific journal articles, published clinical trials, databases and other resources, scientists were able to use machine learning models to quickly prioritise fruitful research pathways, allowing them to bypass traditional bottlenecks associated with vaccine development and enhance the precision of antigen

title: Safeguard Access, Empower Europe: Launching KR21’s Action Plan to Let Libraries be Libraries
content:
The mission of libraries is under threat. Not just from budget cuts or censorship, but from a “silent” crisis: a legal and market framework that is systematically stripping libraries of their right to function. Libraries are being written out of the digital age. It’s time to write them back in.

Today, Knowledge Rights 21 (KR21) launches a definitive roadmap to fight back: Safeguard Access, Empower Europe – An Action Plan to Let Libraries be Libraries.
creator: Stephen Wyber
sourceDescription: We advocate for your right to access and share knowledge!

title: The Political Science of Open Science
content:
This article summarises the results of a round-table discussion at the Science|Business annual conference held in Brussels on 4-5 February 2026. We are grateful for the engagement of other participants whose ideas have shaped what is presented here.

The European Research Area (ERA) Act, now due to be published in the 4th quarter of 2026, has been promoted as the main legislative game in town when it comes to making a reality of the freedom of circulation of knowledge throughout the EU. Advancing open access and open science are central to this goal.

While the ERA Act is historic as the first general research-focused piece of legislation tabled by the European Commission, there has been plenty of other work already in this space. Through fundin
creator: Stephen Wyber

title: Opening Research Information by Law?
content:
For over two decades, the open access movement has advocated for—and delivered on—the goal of making the published results of research available without financial barriers.

Building on this, the drive for open science has expanded this focus, recognising that full openness goes beyond publishing research findings to encompass the full range of outputs generated throughout the research process, including data, methodologies, and more.

However, openness does not stop at the information produced in the pursuit of research: it also covers research information – information about research itself. This is at the heart of the Barcelona Declaration, agreed in Apri
creator: Stephen Wyber
sourceDescription: We advocate for your right to access and share knowledge!

title: Rights? Wrong! 4 Problems That Tougher Copyright Law Won’t Solve
content:
Copyright was created as a means of ensuring that once someone had created a work—originally a book but now also a song, picture, or some other form of creation—they had the benefit of a principle that applied to the control of its distribution and use. At least if people wanted to buy it, they would then need to pay, creating an income stream for the creator or whoever else held the rights.

It has given rise to business models that, as its advocates argue, represent a noteworthy share of the economy—on average an estimated 4% across Europe, depending on how broadly the creative sector is defined. It has allowed creators and those who help them bring their works to the public, as well as those who manage rights professionally, to make a living and more. In addition, it cre
creator: Stephen Wyber
sourceDescription: We advocate for your right to access and share knowledge!
